Spring & Summer 2026

Building Our Garden

A layered, intentional outdoor space — designed for beauty, safe for the whole family, and rooted in the plants and rhythms of Portland.

123 Garden Street · Example Neighborhood · Portland, OR

The Vision

What We're Creating

This isn't just landscaping — it's building a space that evolves with us. A front yard that draws the eye with layered purples and sculptural maples. A backyard that feels like a private, lush retreat with tropical cannas and a shaded pergola. Everything safe for the dog and the baby, low-maintenance once established, and full of things we can eat.

60+
Plants across all zones

4
Seasons of interest

Our Color Story

The Garden Palette

Cool purples and blues greet the street; the backyard shifts warmer with tropical oranges, pinks, and chartreuse. The transition from front to back creates a sense of moving from composed formality into a lush private retreat.

The Property

Our ~3,500 sqft Canvas

Hover over any zone to see what's planted there. The street faces east (top of map), the back fence faces west (bottom). No true south-facing zones — we work with east and west exposure.

Hover over any zone for details · East (street) at top

Zone Tour

The Front Yard

This is our public face — what the neighbors see, what sets the tone when you pull up. The palette here is intentionally cool: layered purples, blues, and lavenders from the lilac, catmint, iris, and geraniums, all grounded by the deep burgundy of two Japanese maples. White Fothergilla blooms in spring add brightness, and it delivers again in fall with stunning orange-red foliage.

Fothergilla 'Mt. Airy'

Fothergilla major

Our big front-yard addition — replacing the pampas grass, which had to go (sharp leaves, injury risk). Mt. Airy earns its spot year-round: fragrant white bottlebrush blooms in spring, clean green summer foliage, then a show-stopping fall display in orange, red, and gold. Native, low-maintenance, and completely non-toxic.

New Addition Statement Plant Family Safe

Desert King Fig

Ficus carica 'Desert King'

Bold, sculptural, and surprisingly well-suited to Portland. The large lobed leaves create instant Mediterranean character, and it produces sweet green figs without needing a pollinator. Low maintenance, minimal pest issues, and the fruit is a bonus. A living focal point.

New Addition Statement Plant Edible

Preston Lilac

Syringa x prestoniae

Anchors the street-side bed with height and those classic purple-lavender blooms. Fragrant and tough.

Existing

Japanese Maple

Acer palmatum

Two specimens provide year-round burgundy structure. The backbone of the front yard's color contrast.

Existing Anchor

Geranium 'Rozanne'

Geranium 'Gerwat'

Violet-blue flowers that bloom from June straight through to frost. The longest-blooming perennial in the garden.

Fall Addition

For Discussion: Front Yard Color & Style

The front yard is currently very cool-toned — lots of purples and blues. Does this palette feel right, or would you want a warm accent somewhere? The dahlia color choice is our lever — we could go warm (coral, orange, deep red) for a pop, or stay cool (purple, white) to keep the monochromatic elegance.

North Side Shade Bed

A narrow, heavily shaded corridor along the north wall. This is where the hydrangeas live — and they're coming back strong after their first-year dieback. Lush sword ferns thrive in the deep shade, and we're adding sweet woodruff as fragrant groundcover where the dead azalea was.

Hydrangea 'Nikko Blue'

Hydrangea macrophylla

Classic blue mopheads. Coming back strong after year-one dieback — great sign. Paired with 'All Summer Beauty' for extended bloom.

Existing Monitor Access

Sweet Woodruff

Galium odoratum

Fragrant groundcover with delicate white star-shaped flowers. Replacing the dead azalea — perfect for deep shade. Hay-like scent when dried.

New Addition Family Safe

Safety Note: North Side Access

The hydrangeas are toxic if ingested (all parts — causes GI upset and lethargy). Our plan is to control access to this side yard rather than remove them — it's a narrow corridor that's easy to gate off. The ferns and sweet woodruff are completely safe.

The Backyard Retreat

This is where the garden shifts — from composed streetside cool tones into a warm, tropical-feeling private space. The patio is our outdoor living room, anchored by dramatic cannas in those blue ceramic pots, a clematis-covered pergola, and the rosemary bonsai.

Patio & Container Layout

Patio container and structure layout · House wall at top

Canna 'Tropicanna'

Canna x generalis

The showstopper. Wild, multi-colored striped foliage — green, yellow, red, and pink — topped with vivid orange flowers. Going in the largest blue ceramic pot (Ceramic B) at the edge of the slab, creating a focal point that draws the eye from the back door across the patio.

New Addition Statement Plant

Canna 'Tropicanna Black'

Canna x generalis

The dramatic counterpart. Deep burgundy-black foliage with orange-red flowers. Ceramic A, positioned inside the gazebo under filtered light. Together with Tropicanna, they form two points of the visual focal triangle — the third being the apricot tree along the back fence.

New Addition Statement Plant

Clematis 'Bees Jubilee'

Clematis (Group 2)

Already massive — owns half the pergola canopy. Pink petals with a carmine bar. The honeysuckle will eventually vine across the other half for complete shade.

Existing Anchor

Honeysuckle 'Serotina'

Lonicera periclymenum

Coming back slowly — it'll eventually cover the remaining pergola half. Cream and deep red flowers, intensely fragrant in the evenings. The hammock dream.

Returning

Hosta 'Sum and Substance'

Hosta

Massive chartreuse-gold leaves in a pot by the front door. Pairs beautifully with the burgundy dwarf maple — warm gold against deep red.

New Addition

Puget Gold Apricot

Prunus armeniaca

Year two and already flowering! Currently potted in the arborvitae gap along the back fence. The third point of our visual focal triangle.

Year 2 Edible

The Edible Corner

Rosemary bonsai (in-ground, 4x4ft) · 5-tier strawberry tower · Herb ladder (oregano, thyme, sage, chives, cilantro, parsley) · Basil in a dark heat-absorbing pot · 3x productive blueberry bushes out front

All Edible Family Safe

The Pollinator Garden

Container Bed 2 was mostly empty — perfect opportunity. We're transforming it into a dedicated pollinator garden with a classic purple-pink-white gradient. Bees, butterflies, and hummingbirds will love this corner. Positioned away from the main seating area so the pollinators do their thing without bothering anyone.

Salvia 'Caradonna'

Salvia nemorosa

Deep violet-purple spikes on near-black stems. Architectural, drought-tolerant, and a magnet for pollinators. Blooms June through August.

New Addition

Agastache 'Blue Fortune'

Agastache

Lavender-blue flower spikes with a light anise scent. Hummingbird favorite. Bridges the gap between the salvia's deep purple and the echinacea's pink.

New Addition

Echinacea 'Magnus'

Echinacea purpurea

Classic pink coneflower with a prominent orange cone. The warm anchor in the pollinator trio — butterflies can't resist it. Seed heads feed birds in winter.

New Addition

The Lawn Conversion

The biggest single project this season. The backyard is currently a sea of river rocks — we're converting it to a soft, walkable surface that's safe for the baby to play on and makes dog cleanup actually possible. The answer: Fleur de Lawn, a micro-clover and flower mix that stays low, handles foot traffic, and looks beautiful.

The Transformation

Now

River rocks everywhere. Hard to walk on, impossible for baby to crawl on, dog waste nightmare.

→
After

Fleur de Lawn micro-clover mix. Soft, green, walkable. Small white flowers. Low-mow, drought-tolerant.

Walkway Concept

The river rocks don't go to waste — we'll arrange them into intentional decorative walkway paths through the clover, creating structure and visual interest.

Family Safety

Dog & Baby Safe

Every plant has been checked for toxicity. The vast majority of our garden is completely safe. A few plants need managed access — here's the full picture.

Managed Access (North Side Yard)

  • Hydrangeas — all parts toxic if ingested (GI upset, lethargy). Plan: gate off the narrow north side corridor.
  • Pacific Bleeding Heart (SW corner) — all parts contain alkaloids. In the far back corner, away from regular traffic.

Completely Safe Zones

All culinary herbs, blueberries, strawberries, ferns, fothergilla, salvias, echinacea, agastache, alyssum, succulents, arborvitae, sweet woodruff, clover lawn, and the rosemary bonsai are fully non-toxic. The main living areas — patio, slab, lawn zone, front yard — are all family safe.

Investment

Budget & Timeline

Estimated Total

~$1,000

Soil, plants, hardware, seed · Spring through Fall 2026

The Season Ahead

March — Prep

Soil delivery from Local Soil Supplier. Start basil indoors. Trim iris, clean beds. Fothergilla goes in. Start rock gathering for walkways.

April — Plant

Main planting push: sweet woodruff, pollinator garden (salvia, agastache, echinacea). Herbs refreshed. Lawn conversion begins — soil spread, clover seed down.

May — Establish

Cannas go in once it's warm. Basil moves outside. Strawberry runners planted. First blooms appear. Clover germinates.

June – August — Enjoy

Harvest season: strawberries, blueberries, herbs. Full bloom across all zones. Water management, deadheading, basil pinching. The garden is alive.

September – October — Transition

Allium bulbs planted for next spring. Fothergilla and maples put on their fall show. Canna cutting, mulching, overwinter prep.

Over to You

Let's Talk About It

Everything above is the plan as it stands — but your eye for design and style is what makes this a home, not just a yard. Here are some things I'd love your input on.

Open Questions

  • Does the front yard's cool purple/blue palette feel right, or do you want warmer accents?
  • What color dahlia speaks to you? (This is our best lever for tuning the front yard vibe)
  • How do you feel about the tropical canna look for the backyard? (Bold striped foliage, orange flowers)
  • The blue ceramic pots are our main containers — do they work, or would you prefer a different material/color?
  • Any zones that feel under-designed or could use more attention?
  • Are there plants you've always wanted that we haven't considered?

This is our garden. Let's make it ours.

Spring 2026 · Portland, OR
